page.title: The Server
page.description: Personal introduction and professional overview: the engineer behind the portfolio — how editorial design became web structure, and how that foundation shows up in frontend architecture, design systems, and product platforms.

hero.title: The Server
hero.subtitle: Personal Introduction / Professional Overview
hero.lead: Hi, my name is Victor Zamudio. I’ll be your server from here.
hero.intro: In Spanish, tu servidor means at your service. This is the personal introduction to the engineer behind the portfolio—and the starting point for understanding the work, judgment, and systems presented throughout it.

labels.core: Personal introduction and professional overview: the summary view, the person, how editorial structure became web structure, and the engineer behind the portfolio
labels.impact: The impact of an engineering practice founded on editorial structure, design–engineering fluency, and system-level thinking

items: Editorial design established the structural foundation of my web practice.
I work across frontend architecture, design systems, product platforms, and technical alignment.
I combine visual judgment, semantic structure, engineering quality, and system-level thinking.
This portfolio separates that identity into evolution, evidence, methods, and direction.

primaryContent.title: Hello, Friend
paragraphs: I was born and raised in Mexico and remain based in Mexico City—my home, my context, and my beloved country.

I am a Staff Software Engineer at Thomson Reuters, where I have worked since September 2022. I have been building for the web since June 2006, contributing across enterprise, media, fintech, healthcare, and aviation product environments.
The complete career progression belongs to The Navigator. What matters here is the connection between where I started and how I operate today.

primaryContent.title: Editorial Structure Became Web Structure
paragraphs: I studied Graphic Communication Design at Universidad Autónoma Metropolitana, specializing in Editorial Design. During my final year, I worked on the university’s design publication with grids, prototypes, indexes, covers, headers, footers, articles, content hierarchy, and editorial markup. Those practices became the first foundations of my work on the web. [1]
The relationship is literal at the level of document structure.

Editorial markup identifies the role of content before deciding how it should look: title, heading, article, paragraph, caption, list, or reference. HTML applies the same structural principle to web documents. The W3C describes HTML as semantic markup created to describe documents, and historically defines it as an SGML application for structured document types. [2]

diagram.title: Diagram 01 — From Editorial Markup to Product Architecture
diagram.description: Shows how an editorial foundation expanded into increasingly complex engineering systems.
diagram.caption: The medium changed, but the core discipline remained: identify meaning, establish hierarchy, and create a structure others can navigate.
diagram.summary: A top-to-bottom progression: Editorial Markup, then Semantic HTML, then Accessible Interfaces, then Reusable Components, then Design Systems, then Product Architecture.

primaryContent.closingStatement: Editorial design taught me the principle before I learned the syntax: structure gives content meaning; presentation gives that meaning form.

primaryContent.title: The Engineer Behind the Portfolio
paragraphs: Today, I work where interface decisions become shared product systems.

My scope includes frontend architecture, component systems, design-system integration, accessibility, engineering standards, API collaboration, and technical direction across teams. I connect product intent with implementation constraints and help turn individual decisions into patterns that others can understand, adopt, and extend.
The portfolio presents that professional identity through distinct forms of evidence rather than repeating it on every page.

diagram.title: Diagram 02 — The Professional Picture
diagram.description: Shows how the portfolio reveals one engineering identity through separate but connected perspectives.
diagram.caption: The Server introduces the person; the rest of the portfolio demonstrates the engineer.
diagram.summary: A top-to-bottom progression through the portfolio: The Server, then The Navigator, then Selected Work, then The Pattern, The Assembly, The Map and The Signal, then Future Direction.

stages.name: The Pattern · The Assembly · The Map · The Signal

items.title: Structural Clarity
items.description: Editorial hierarchy and semantic markup help me organize interfaces around meaning rather than visual treatment alone.

items.title: Design–Engineering Fluency
items.description: I connect product and visual intent with accessibility, component behavior, architecture, and implementation quality.

items.title: Product-System Awareness
items.description: I evaluate interface decisions through their effects on shared capabilities, teams, adoption, and future change.

items.title: Engineering Leverage
items.description: I turn individual contributions into reusable patterns, preserved reasoning, and clearer technical direction.

items.title: The Navigator
items.description: Traces the progression from editorial design and early web practice to Staff Software Engineering.

items.title: The Integrator
items.description: Shows how I connect product needs, design intent, frontend architecture, APIs, and engineering teams.

items.title: Thomson Reuters
items.description: Provides current evidence across product platforms, shared UI systems, accessibility, quality, and AI-enabled initiatives.

items.title: Nobody Knows How He Thinks...
items.description: Introduces the technical models behind my work in design systems, component architecture, product architecture, and AI integration.

closingThought.statement: Before I engineered product systems, editorial design taught me their first principle: structure makes meaning visible.
